problem_summary: 2018 MCM/ICM Problem B (discrete). The team (control #73410, Outstanding award) had to (1) project the global geographic distribution of language speakers 50 years out (to 2067 from a 2017 base), explicitly modeling native vs. non-native speakers and migration, and (2) advise a multinational service corporation on where to open 6 new international offices so they cover the major language regions optimally. The paper's spine is a custom block-structured "inhomogeneous transition matrix" (a time-varying Markov-like population model) feeding a simulated-annealing combinatorial optimizer for office siting.
models_used: Discrete-time linear population dynamics modeled as a Markov-chain-style transition matrix Y^(n+1)=A·Y^(n); steady state read off as the dominant eigenvalue/eigenvector (A·Y=λY) of A, where λ is the long-run growth rate and the normalized eigenvector is the equilibrium population split

Two-stage modeling: a 'Preliminary Model' (homogeneous constant matrix A, 22 languages, 4×4 per-language block A·Y for a 44×44 system) validated against real 2017 data, then a 'Modified Model' that adds time-dependence

Inhomogeneous (time-varying) transition matrix A^(n)=F[Y^(n),n] with a block structure: Ξ (native natural-growth, diagonal 1+growth), Π/Π1 (non-native decrease = 1−death−migration), Ω (migrant death), G/G1 (learning matrices, native→non-native), R/R1 (migration matrices, non-native→migrant), S (migrant reproduction) — separate English (subscript-1) blocks to track non-native English distribution

Mechanistic learning-rate sub-model: γ_ji^(n) = p_ji·a_i / (σ_i·d_ji), where p=eagerness (∝ current non-native population), a=new-learner proportion, σ=years to learn, d=language-family similarity (1 same family, 2 different)

Office-siting scoring/objective function S_i = ε^{k_i} · Σ_j (P_ij^η1 · E_ij^η2 / D_ij^η3) − h_i, a gravity-model-style score trading population P, GDP E, transport cost D, with a multiplicative per-extra-language penalty ε^{k_i} and fixed establishment cost h_i

Simulated Annealing (Metropolis–Hastings acceptance) to solve the discrete language-to-city assignment optimization (T0=97, Tt=3, cooling α=0.99, Markov chain length 10000), run 10 times for a stable solution
Economic cycle GDP forecast: superposition of Kitchin, Juglar, Kuznets, and Kondratiev cycles fit as a trigonometric function to ~46 years of GDP data, used to project 2067 regional GDP feeding E_ij
key_techniques: Disaggregating 'speakers' into native, non-native, learners, and migrants as separate state compartments rather than one population number — a compartmental redefinition of the problem
Block-matrix construction so each demographic process (birth, death, learning, migration, reproduction of migrants) lives in its own clearly-named sub-matrix, making a 44×44+ system legible
Eigenvalue/eigenvector interpretation given concrete meaning (λ = long-run growth rate, eigenvector = equilibrium share) and then used as a diagnostic that exposed a model flaw

Using the preliminary model's wrong long-run answer (Panjabi → 96% of population because highest birth rate dominates a homogeneous chain) as explicit motivation to upgrade to an inhomogeneous, time-varying matrix — turning a failure into the narrative bridge between models
Parameterizing soft real-world factors (government policy, economy, education) by mapping them onto specific model parameters (p_ij, r_j, σ_i, a_i)
Validating projections against ground truth: predicted vs. real 2017 native/non-native counts in a side-by-side table, and openly attributing residuals to bad source data
Sensitivity analysis via a single tunable exponent k on eagerness (γ ∝ (y_i)^k) sweeping 'uniformity of language choice' from k=0.1 to k=10 to show regime change in English vs Mandarin dominance

Switching the optimization weights (η1,η2,η3 and ε) to answer different business framings — necessities (population-weighted) vs non-necessities (GDP-weighted) — producing multiple tailored office maps
why_it_won: "It reframed a vague forecasting prompt into a clean mechanistic state-transition model with genuine mathematical content (eigen-analysis with real interpretation), then connected that forecast to a real decision via a second, different technique (SA combinatorial optimization) — demonstrating breadth (modeling + optimization + econometrics) and an end-to-end pipeline. Crucially it showed scientific honesty: it built a simple model first, validated it against real 2017 data, diagnosed why it failed in the long run via the eigenvector, and used that to justify the more sophisticated inhomogeneous model — exactly the iterative narrative judges reward. The block-matrix decomposition and named sub-matrices made an intimidating 44×44 system readable, and the answers were decision-ready (concrete city lists, 6-vs-5 office tradeoff, weight scenarios), capped with a client memo that translated math into business advice."
section_highlights: "Strong structural moves: (1) Two-model arc — Preliminary (constant matrix) then Modified (inhomogeneous), with a clear, explicit list of the preliminary model's 5 defects bridging them. (2) Worked N=2 toy example with a transition diagram before scaling to N=22/26 — eases the reader in. (3) Validation table (predicted vs real 2017 counts) placed early to earn trust, with candid discussion of the German non-native anomaly and data-quality caveats. (4) Eigen-interpretation given plain-English meaning right where computed. (5) Sensitivity analysis isolated to one interpretable knob (k) with two contrasting regimes (k=0.1 vs k=10). (6) Application section reuses model outputs as inputs to the SA optimizer, and reports both 2017 and 2067 solutions plus a 6-vs-5 office economic tradeoff. (7) Client memo at the end restates findings in business language with three pricing-model-specific office maps. Notation tables precede each model section; strengths/weaknesses are explicit and self-critical."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Parameter estimation is largely hand-set or assumed (a_i=0.01, σ_i=3, similarity d∈{1,2}, ε=0.98, η=1) rather than calibrated/fitted, so the 2067 numbers rest on convenient guesses — limited rigor in parameter justification

The headline conclusion (English overtakes Mandarin) is highly sensitive to the eagerness-uniformity exponent k, which the sensitivity analysis itself reveals — yet the team still reports a single confident ranking in the memo

Data quality is weak and acknowledged: visible inconsistencies (non-native German jumps 8M→52M; Russian non-native 27 vs 113; Japanese non-native left as '?'), so validation residuals are large for several languages

The learning/eagerness sub-model is somewhat circular: eagerness p is assumed proportional to current non-native population, which biases toward already-popular languages (built-in rich-get-richer for English)

Feedback is only partial — they admit population structure should feed back on birth/death/migration but only model its effect on learning; migrants' own first language influencing destination regions is ignored

The SA setup is under-justified (T0=97, Tt=3, α=0.99, chain length 10000 given without tuning rationale) and 'run 10 times' is a weak convergence argument; no comparison to an exact/enumeration baseline at small scale
The economic 4-cycle trigonometric GDP fit (Kitchin/Juglar/Kuznets/Kondratiev) is an ambitious add-on with little validation and produces implausibly large 50-year GDP multiples
reusable_patterns: Compartmentalize a population/quantity into mechanistically distinct sub-groups, then encode each transition process as a named block of a single transition matrix — gives a legible large linear system and easy eigen-analysis

Build a simple homogeneous version first, validate against real data, and let its long-run failure mode (dominant-eigenvalue domination) explicitly motivate the time-inhomogeneous upgrade — a ready-made two-model narrative arc
Give λ and the dominant eigenvector concrete domain meaning (growth rate / equilibrium share) and use them as a sanity check that can expose modeling errors
Two-stage pipeline: a forecasting model produces inputs (population, GDP) that feed a separate optimization model for the decision — demonstrates breadth and ties math to an actual recommendation

Gravity-model objective P^η·E^η/D^η with a multiplicative penalty term (ε^k) for over-coverage and a subtracted fixed cost h, solved by Simulated Annealing with Metropolis acceptance — a transplantable template for facility-location / assignment problems
Map qualitative exogenous factors (policy, economy, education) onto specific named parameters so 'soft' drivers become tunable knobs
Run the optimizer under multiple weight regimes to answer different stakeholder framings (e.g., population-weighted vs GDP-weighted) and present several tailored solutions instead of one
Do sensitivity analysis through a single interpretable exponent that sweeps a real-world spectrum (here: uniformity of choice) and show regime changes
Close with a client memo that re-expresses the math as plain business recommendations, including an explicit cost/benefit tradeoff (6 vs 5 offices) and conditional advice
